---
title: "Part V. Optimization, Tuning, and Diagnostics"
---

::: {.part-hero}
## Part V

This part makes Bayesian workflow operational: optimization, tuning, convergence assessment, model criticism, and predictive diagnostics.
:::

## Part Overview

Part V makes the course operational. Bayesian methods are only as credible as their optimization, tuning, diagnostics, and model criticism practices.

## Part Learning Goals

By the end of Part V, learners should be able to:

- connect MAP estimation to optimization and regularization;
- tune HMC, MCMC, and variational methods deliberately;
- diagnose convergence and model misfit using modern Bayesian workflow tools;
- communicate what diagnostics can and cannot certify.

## Fully Written Chapters In This Repository

- [Optimization techniques in Bayesian statistics](../chapters/practice/optimization-techniques-in-bayesian-statistics.qmd)
- [Tuning and calibration](../chapters/practice/tuning-and-calibration.qmd)
- [Diagnostics and model criticism](../chapters/practice/diagnostics-and-model-criticism.qmd)

## Reading Path

Recommended order:

1. [Optimization techniques in Bayesian statistics](../chapters/practice/optimization-techniques-in-bayesian-statistics.qmd)
2. [Tuning and calibration](../chapters/practice/tuning-and-calibration.qmd)
3. [Diagnostics and model criticism](../chapters/practice/diagnostics-and-model-criticism.qmd)

## Chapter 21. Optimization Techniques in Bayesian Statistics

### Core question

When does posterior mode optimization help, and how does it relate to full Bayesian inference?

### Section blueprint

- MAP estimation.
- Penalized likelihood connections.
- Gradient descent and Newton-Raphson.
- Quasi-Newton and coordinate descent.
- Stochastic gradient methods.
- Natural-gradient overview.
- Constrained optimization and Lagrange multipliers.

### Derivations that must appear

- MAP as maximization of log likelihood plus log prior.
- Newton step from second-order Taylor expansion.
- Coordinate descent idea in separable penalties.
- Constrained MAP via Lagrangian formulation.

### Computational lab

- Compute MAP for logistic regression in `R` and `JAX`.
- Compare MAP, posterior mean, and Laplace approximation.
- Examine sensitivity to optimization initialization in non-convex settings.

### Common pitfalls

- Treating MAP as a substitute for posterior uncertainty.
- Ignoring posterior curvature when reporting only point estimates.
- Applying Newton methods without checking Hessian stability.

### Exercises

- Derive the MAP objective for Bayesian linear regression.
- Compute one Newton step for logistic regression.

### Core references

- @mackay2003
- @murphy2023
- @bishop2006

## Chapter 22. Tuning and Calibration

### Core question

How should algorithmic hyperparameters be chosen so that computation is stable, efficient, and diagnostically trustworthy?

### Section blueprint

- Tuning proposal scales in MCMC.
- Choosing warmup, chain length, and number of chains.
- Stan controls: `adapt_delta`, `max_treedepth`, and initialization.
- Selecting variational families and optimization schedules.
- Tuning gradient-based optimizers in JAX.
- Hyperparameter sensitivity versus prior sensitivity.

### Derivations that must appear

- Acceptance rate trade-offs in random-walk proposals.
- ESS and Monte Carlo error as functions of autocorrelation.
- Step-size stability intuition for gradient methods.

### Computational lab

- Tune a random-walk Metropolis sampler and compare ESS per second.
- Fit the same Stan model under several `adapt_delta` settings.
- Compare learning-rate schedules for a JAX MAP optimizer.

### Common pitfalls

- Using longer chains instead of fixing poor geometry.
- Overfitting optimizer hyperparameters to one data set.
- Confusing computational tuning with model specification.

### Exercises

- Explain when increasing `max_treedepth` helps and when it only masks problems.
- Compare fixed and adaptive proposal scales on a skewed target.

### Core references

- @hoffman2014
- @betancourt2017
- @vehtari2021

## Chapter 23. Diagnostics and Model Criticism

### Core question

How do we determine whether posterior computation is trustworthy and whether the model is scientifically adequate?

### Section blueprint

- Prior predictive checks.
- Posterior predictive checks.
- Residual diagnostics.
- Simulation-based calibration.
- $\hat{R}$, ESS, trace plots, and autocorrelation.
- Divergences and E-BFMI.
- WAIC and PSIS-LOO.
- Bayes factors, stacking, and model averaging.
- Sensitivity analysis.

### Derivations that must appear

- Posterior predictive discrepancy construction.
- SBC rank-uniformity intuition.
- ESS from autocorrelation sums.
- PSIS-LOO rationale and importance-weight stabilization overview.

### Computational lab

- Posterior predictive checks in `bayesplot`.
- Compute PSIS-LOO and compare to WAIC.
- Diagnose divergences in a hierarchical model and trace them to geometry.
- Run a small SBC experiment for a simple model.

### Common pitfalls

- Treating $\hat{R} < 1.01$ as a complete guarantee of correctness.
- Using predictive criteria without checking observation-wise diagnostics.
- Relying on Bayes factors without prior sensitivity analysis.
- Ignoring prior predictive absurdities because posterior fit looks acceptable.

### Exercises

- Compute ESS from an autocorrelation sequence.
- Design a posterior predictive discrepancy for count overdispersion.
- Explain what uniform SBC ranks would mean in a well-calibrated workflow.

### Core references

- @gelman1992
- @vehtari2017
- @vehtari2021
- @gabry2019
